content:
First we build a working API. Then we define REST properly. Then we take the whole thing apart — Part 5 of Feel the Pain First

7 min read
Just now

--

Let’s build something real first, then talk about it.
@RestController
@RequestMapping("/users")
public class UserController { private final Map<Integer, User> users = new HashMap<>();
@GetMapping("/{id}")
public User getUser(@PathVariable int id) {
return users.getOrDefault(id, new User(id, "Unknown"));
}
@PostMapping
public User createUser(@RequestBody User user) {
users.put(user.getId(), user);
return user;
}
}
$ curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/users -d '{"id":1,"name":"Rajat"}' -H "Content-Type: application/json"
{"id":1,"name":"Rajat"}
$ curl http://localhost:8080/users/1
{"id":1,"name":"Rajat"}Four annotations did all the visible work: @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @PathVariable, @RequestBody. Before taking any of them apart, one question actually comes first — what makes this a REST API, rather than just some HTTP endpoints that happen to return JSON?
What REST actually is
REST — Representational State Transfer — isn’t a library or a protocol. It’s a set of conventions from Roy Fielding’s 2000 doctoral dissertation, and the name itself is worth pulling apart, because every word in it is doing real work:

• Representational — the client and server never share the actual resource, only a representation of it (JSON, XML, whatever format you choose). The real User object lives on the server; what crosses the network is a description of it.

• State — a resource has state — a User has an id and a name, right now, at this moment. That state is what's being represented.

• Transfer — that representation of the current state gets transferred from server to client (or client to server) over HTTP. Nothing more mystical than that.
So, read plainly: REST means describing the current state of something as data, and shipping that description back and forth over HTTP. Everything else — resources, verbs, statelessness — is really just the detailed rulebook for how to do that consistently.
Rule 1: everything is a resource, named as a noun
/users/1 names a thing. It never names an action — no /getUser, no /createUser. What you're allowed to do to that thing comes from somewhere else entirely: the HTTP method.
Rule 2: the HTTP method carries the verb
The same URL means something different depending on how you reach it:

• GET /users/1 → read it

• PUT /users/1 → replace it

• DELETE /users/1 → remove it

• POST /users → create a new one
This is exactly why getUser and createUser above map to the same resource — they're two verbs on one noun, not two unrelated features. It's called CRUD-to-HTTP: Create → POST, Read → GET, Update → PUT/PATCH, Delete → DELETE.
Rule 3: statelessness — the constraint that actually matters most
Every request must carry everything the server needs — no memory of previous requests kept between calls. This is precisely why Chapter 1’s count++ bug can't happen in a properly stateless endpoint: there's no shared field left over from a previous request to race on. It's also why REST APIs scale horizontally so easily — any server can handle any request, since none of them are holding onto client-specific memory.
Rule 4: JSON was never part of the definition
Look back at the breakdown above — the word is representational, not “JSON-al.” A resource can be represented as JSON, XML, even HTML, and REST doesn’t care which. JSON just happens to be the convention that won, for reasons Chapter 4 covers in detail.
The one-sentence version
A REST API exposes the current state of named resources, transferred as representations, addressed by URL, manipulated via the standard meaning of HTTP methods, with every request self-contained.
With that settled, every annotation above has a meaning, not just a mechanism — now let’s find the mechanism too.
Back to where this series actually started
Chapter 1 gave you this, by hand:
response.setContentType("application/json");
response.getWriter().println("{\"id\": " + id + ", \"name\": \"" + name + "\"}");Three facts worth holding onto: response is an object Tomcat built for this one request, wired internally to the actual network socket. getWriter() returns a PrintWriter tied to that socket — whatever text you hand it gets sent, verbatim, as the response body, with zero translation. And the JSON string itself was entirely your own manual work — the Servlet API has no concept of JSON; you typed out every brace and quote by hand.
In Spring, you never call getWriter(). You never build that string. So who does, and how?
Three jobs, and only three
Getting from a returned Java object to JSON bytes on a socket is exactly three separable jobs, and mixing them up is the most common source of confusion when something breaks:

• Interception — something has to call your controller method and receive the returned object, since you never call it yourself.

• Conversion — something has to turn that object into a representation (JSON, by default) — a job that needs zero knowledge of HTTP, sockets, or servlets.

• Transmission — something has to set the right header and call getWriter() with the finished text.
Every annotation in this story exists to automate exactly one of these three.
Job 1: DispatcherServlet is a servlet you already understand
Here’s the detail that makes the rest of this click: DispatcherServlet is a real subclass of HttpServlet — the exact class every servlet in Chapter 1 extended. When a Spring Boot app starts, exactly one servlet gets registered with Tomcat. Every URL, every request, arrives at this one servlet's methods — the same doGet/doPost machinery from Chapter 1, just written once by the Spring team instead of once per endpoint by every developer, forever.
So the request’s journey starts exactly where Chapter 1 left off: Tomcat accepts the connection, builds HttpServletRequest/HttpServletResponse wired to that socket, and calls DispatcherServlet — because it's the only servlet registered for any URL.
DispatcherServlet now needs to know which of your methods handles this specific (HTTP method, URL) combination. @GetMapping/@PostMapping are passive metadata — the same kind of inert flag @Component is for @ComponentScan. At startup, Spring scans every @RestController class (found via @ComponentScan, exactly as in Chapter 2) and builds a lookup table: (method, URL pattern) → method reference. @RequestMapping("/users") on the class just contributes a shared prefix to every mapping inside it.
A GET /users/1 comes in. DispatcherServlet searches this table and finds a match against GET /users/{id} — this is the CRUD-to-HTTP idea from before, now visible as an actual searchable data structure instead of a convention in your head.
Two more annotations fill in the method call itself. @PathVariable int id tells Spring: take whatever matched {id} in the URL, convert it to an int, and pass it as this argument — the direct automated replacement for Chapter 1's manual request.getParameter("id") followed by a hand-written Integer.parseInt wrapped in try/catch. @RequestBody User user runs the mirror-image job on the way in: convert the raw incoming JSON text into a User object, before your method body executes at all.
With everything resolved, DispatcherServlet calls your method itself — on the controller instance it holds, which is a bean built by the IoC container exactly as Chapter 2 described — and receives whatever object comes back. Job 1, solved, entirely by a lookup table and a servlet you already knew existed.
Job 2: @ResponseBody and the converter
DispatcherServlet is now holding a User object, and has to decide what it means. In traditional Spring MVC — used for server-rendered HTML — a return value was historically treated as the name of a view to render, a template to look up and fill in. That's not what a REST API wants: we want the object's current state sent back as a representation, not turned into a page.
@ResponseBody is the flag that flips this behavior: "don't render a view, convert this and write it directly to the response body." @RestController is nothing more than @Controller + @ResponseBody, bundled into one annotation — the same convenience-wrapper trick @SpringBootApplication used back in Chapter 1.
Once flagged, the object is handed to an HttpMessageConverter — an interface whose entire job is: object in, formatted bytes and a content type out. This interface is the concrete mechanism behind "representations aren't part of REST's definition" — swap which converter is active, and the exact same UserController, unmodified, starts returning XML instead of JSON. Spring Boot auto-configures a Jackson-backed converter the instant it detects Jackson on the classpath, using the same @ConditionalOnClass gate behind DataSourceAutoConfiguration back in Chapter 3 — just pointed at a serialization library instead of a database driver.
The converter reflectively inspects the User object's fields, produces {"id": 1, "name": "Rajat"}, and hands the string back to DispatcherServlet. Job 2, solved: a flag choosing "convert" over "render," and an auto-configured converter doing the actual translation.
Job 3: nothing was replaced — it just changed hands
DispatcherServlet now holds a finished JSON string and knows its content type. The very last step is, character for character, Chapter 1's own code:
response.setContentType("application/json");
response.getWriter().write(jsonString);Same method, same object, same underlying mechanism. The only thing that changed across four chapters of this series is who is making the call — you, by hand, in Chapter 1; DispatcherServlet, automatically, now — and what string it's holding — one you typed, versus one Jackson generated.
The full trace, start to finish
Tomcat’s accept-thread wakes on a new connection and hands the socket to a worker thread. That thread builds HttpServletRequest/HttpServletResponse and calls DispatcherServlet, the only servlet Tomcat knows about. DispatcherServlet searches its mapping table, extracts path variables, converts an incoming body if @RequestBody is present, and calls your controller method directly — receiving the returned object in hand. It checks for @ResponseBody, hands the object to an auto-configured Jackson-backed converter, gets a JSON string back, sets the content type, and calls getWriter() — the exact line Chapter 1 wrote by hand. Tomcat flushes the bytes back over the socket.
Nine steps. Every one of them traces to a mechanism this series already built from first principles — nothing here was ever magic, only wiring you hadn’t seen yet.
Next time: what happens when the incoming JSON is malformed, or fails validation — and why your method body never even runs.

This is Part 5 of “Feel the Pain First.” Part 4, on auto-configuration, is here: [ADD LINK]. Follow for Part 6.
